agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic delivery pricing system that adjusts prices based on demand levels. The CEO's description is high-level and intentionally vague, leaving significant ambiguity around guardrails, thresholds, and pricing boundaries.",
    "bounded_context": "Pricing / Rate Management",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "DynaPricing", "definition": "The product name for the real-time demand-based delivery pricing system" },
      { "term": "Demand", "definition": "A measure of delivery request volume relative to available capacity in a given area and time window. Exact measurement method is unspecified." },
      { "term": "Guardrails", "definition": "Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme pricing. Exact thresholds are unspecified." },
      { "term": "Real-time pricing adjustment", "definition": "The system modifies delivery prices dynamically as demand conditions change, without manual intervention." },
      { "term": "Fully automated", "definition": "No human approval is required for individual price changes once the system is configured and running." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery price must increase when demand is high relative to capacity",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a business policy encoding a pricing strategy. A VP could decide to hold prices flat during high demand for strategic reasons (e.g., market entry, customer retention). The direction of price movement is a policy choice, not a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand rises above the configured 'high' threshold, then the computed delivery price must be greater than the base price"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Delivery price must decrease when demand is low relative to capacity",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Same as BR-1 — this is a pricing policy. The business could choose to hold a price floor during low demand rather than drop prices.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand falls below the configured 'low' threshold, then the computed delivery price must be less than the base price"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must occur in real time without manual human intervention",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Automation level is an operational policy. The business could introduce manual approval gates for certain price tiers or regions. A VP could require human-in-the-loop for price changes above a certain percentage.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a change in demand conditions, the system must compute and apply a new price without requiring human approval, within a defined latency SLA"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Price must never exceed an upper guardrail bound",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Unbounded upward pricing can cause regulatory violations (price gouging laws in many jurisdictions), catastrophic reputational damage, and potential legal liability. If the system allows a price above the maximum, the system state is corrupt from a business perspective — the guardrail exists precisely to prevent this. The specific value of the cap is policy, but the existence of a cap is an invariant.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any computed price P and configured maximum price ceiling MAX, assert P <= MAX under all conditions including extreme demand spikes"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Price must never fall below a lower guardrail bound (price floor)",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A delivery price below cost or at zero/negative would represent a corrupted system state — the business loses money on every delivery with no strategic intent. The specific floor value is policy, but the enforcement of a floor is an invariant to prevent below-cost or nonsensical pricing.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any computed price P and configured minimum price floor MIN, assert P >= MIN under all conditions including zero demand"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "The pricing function must be monotonically related to demand — higher demand must never produce a lower price than lower demand, all else being equal",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is the core pricing model assumption. A business could design a non-monotonic model (e.g., loyalty discounts at certain demand thresholds). It is a policy encoding the CEO's stated intent, not a physical or data-integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given two demand levels D1 > D2 with all other inputs held constant, the price at D1 must be >= the price at D2"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The system must have a base/reference price from which dynamic adjustments are computed",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "A base price is a business configuration parameter. The business could switch to a purely algorithmic model with no fixed base. This is an architectural assumption, not a hard constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Every pricing calculation must reference a configured base price for the given delivery type/zone"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "A delivery price must always be a positive monetary value greater than zero",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A zero or negative delivery price is physically nonsensical — the business is providing a service that has real costs. This is a fundamental domain constraint independent of any pricing policy.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any computed delivery price P, assert P > 0"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Demand spikes instantaneously (e.g., weather event, concert ending)", "expected_behavior": "Price increases but is clamped at the upper guardrail. The system must not overshoot or oscillate." },
    { "scenario": "Demand drops to zero in a region", "expected_behavior": "Price decreases but never falls below the price floor. The system must not produce a zero or negative price." },
    { "scenario": "Demand data feed is unavailable or stale", "expected_behavior": "UNSPECIFIED by CEO. Critical gap — should the system hold last known price, revert to base price, or halt automation?" },
    { "scenario": "Two adjacent zones have wildly different demand levels", "expected_behavior": "UNSPECIFIED. Risk of price arbitrage where customers game zone boundaries to get lower prices." },
    { "scenario": "Rapid demand oscillation (demand flipping high/low repeatedly)", "expected_behavior": "UNSPECIFIED. Risk of price flickering confusing customers mid-checkout. Need a dampening or smoothing policy." },
    { "scenario": "Guardrail values are misconfigured (e.g., floor > ceiling)", "expected_behavior": "System must reject invalid guardrail configuration and refuse to operate with contradictory bounds." },
    { "scenario": "Price surge during a natural disaster or emergency", "expected_behavior": "UNSPECIFIED and HIGH RISK. Many jurisdictions have anti-price-gouging laws. The system may need to detect emergency conditions and cap prices differently." },
    { "scenario": "Customer sees one price at quote time but demand changes before confirmation", "expected_behavior": "UNSPECIFIED. Must define whether quoted price is locked or can change. This is a critical UX and trust issue." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Direct revenue impact — dynamic pricing can increase revenue 10-25% in high-demand periods but risks customer churn if perceived as exploitative. Incorrect guardrails could cause below-cost deliveries at scale.",
    "operational": "Requires real-time demand signal infrastructure, pricing engine with low latency, and monitoring/alerting for anomalies. Fully automated means no human safety net — failures propagate instantly.",
    "user": "Customers will experience variable pricing which can erode trust if not communicated transparently. Price transparency and predictability are major satisfaction drivers in logistics.",
    "risk": "HIGH. Regulatory risk from price gouging laws (especially during emergencies). Reputational risk from perceived unfairness. Technical risk from automation without human oversight. Financial risk from guardrail misconfiguration.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "End customers (senders)", "impact": "Direct price variability on every delivery — may feel exploited during surge pricing" },
      { "group": "Couriers/drivers", "impact": "Higher demand periods may correlate with higher pay if pricing flows through to courier compensation — but this is unspecified" },
      { "group": "Operations team", "impact": "Loses manual pricing control; must now monitor an automated system and configure guardrails correctly" },
      { "group": "Finance/Revenue team", "impact": "Revenue becomes less predictable; forecasting models must account for demand elasticity" },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance", "impact": "Must review pricing against local price gouging and consumer protection laws in every operating jurisdiction" },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will receive complaints about price variability; needs talking points and dispute resolution policy" },
      { "group": "Sales/Partnerships (B2B)", "impact": "Enterprise clients with contracted rates may be affected or need exemptions from dynamic pricing" }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 30,
    "reasoning": "The CEO's description is an informal all-hands soundbite, not a specification. It contains no concrete thresholds, no defined demand metrics, no guardrail values, no mention of legal compliance, no price-lock policy, no zone/segmentation rules, and hedging language ('probably', 'some guardrails', 'don't go crazy'). Almost every critical parameter is unspecified. The rules extracted are inferences from vague directional statements. This is insufficient to build a correct system.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Price must be positive (BR-8) — universal domain truth",
      "Prices go up with high demand, down with low demand — explicitly stated direction",
      "System is automated — explicitly stated"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Guardrail specifics — CEO said 'probably' and 'some', indicating guardrails are not yet decided",
      "What constitutes 'demand' — no definition of how demand is measured",
      "Real-time latency requirements — 'real time' is undefined (seconds? minutes?)",
      "Price lock/quote behavior — completely unaddressed",
      "Geographic/zone granularity — unaddressed",
      "Emergency/disaster pricing policy — unaddressed but legally critical",
      "Interaction with existing pricing (contracts, promotions, B2B rates) — unaddressed",
      "Demand data source reliability and fallback behavior — unaddressed"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Guardrails include both an upper ceiling and a lower floor — CEO only mentioned preventing prices from 'going crazy' (upward), but a floor is a necessary business safeguard",
      "A base price exists from which adjustments are computed — not stated but implied by 'adjust'",
      "The pricing applies to the delivery fee component, not the entire order value",
      "Dynamic pricing applies to on-demand deliveries, not pre-contracted or subscription-based deliveries"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The CEO's description provides no definition of 'demand' — is it order volume per hour, orders per available courier, geographic density, or predictive demand? The pricing model cannot be built without a concrete demand metric.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Data Science", "Operations"],
      "options": ["Define demand as orders-per-hour per zone", "Define demand as ratio of orders to available couriers", "Use a composite demand index"],
      "recommendation": "Require Product to define the demand metric formally before any implementation begins."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Guardrail values and even their existence are uncertain — CEO said 'probably' and 'some guardrails'. Are guardrails mandatory? What are the ceiling and floor values? Are they absolute values or percentage multipliers?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Finance", "Customers"],
      "options": ["Treat guardrails as mandatory with configurable values", "Treat guardrails as optional feature flag", "Require Legal review before defining guardrails"],
      "recommendation": "Treat guardrails as MANDATORY (invariant) given regulatory risk. Require Legal to define jurisdiction-specific maximum multipliers before launch."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No mention of price-lock policy. When a customer is quoted a dynamic price, can it change before they confirm? This is a critical trust and UX decision with direct revenue and legal implications.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Legal", "Product", "Customer Support"],
      "options": ["Lock price for N minutes after quote", "Price can change until payment is confirmed", "Show price range instead of exact price"],
      "recommendation": "Lock quoted price for a configurable TTL (e.g., 5 minutes) to maintain customer trust and avoid bait-and-switch complaints."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No mention of emergency/disaster scenarios. Price gouging during emergencies is illegal in many jurisdictions and carries severe reputational risk.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Customers", "Public Relations"],
      "options": ["Implement emergency mode that caps prices at base rate", "Disable dynamic pricing entirely during declared emergencies", "Apply jurisdiction-specific emergency price caps"],
      "recommendation": "Implement an emergency override that freezes prices at or below base rate. This must be in v1 — not a future enhancement."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No fallback behavior defined for when demand data is unavailable, stale, or corrupted. A fully automated system with no human oversight and no fallback is a significant operational risk.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Operations", "Engineering", "Customers"],
      "options": ["Revert to base price when data is stale beyond threshold", "Hold last known price for configurable duration", "Circuit-break to manual pricing mode"],
      "recommendation": "Revert to base price when demand data is stale beyond a configurable threshold. This is a safety-critical fallback."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
